questions.question: Why is CSS considered render-blocking?
options: The browser blocks rendering until CSSOM is built
questions.explanation: The browser blocks page rendering until the CSSOM is fully constructed, making CSS a render-blocking resource.

questions.question: Which CSS properties are GPU-composited?
questions.explanation: transform and opacity are composited on the GPU and don't trigger layout or paint.

questions.question: What is critical CSS?
options: The minimal CSS needed to render above-the-fold content
questions.explanation: Critical CSS is the minimum set of styles required to render the initial viewport content, typically inlined in <head>.

questions.question: What does `content-visibility: auto` do?
questions.explanation: content-visibility: auto skips rendering for elements outside the viewport, improving initial load performance.

questions.question: Which CSS loading method is render-blocking?
questions.explanation: A standard <link> in <head> is render-blocking. Other methods can be used for non-critical CSS.

questions.question: What is a CSS selector performance myth?
options: Selectors are a major performance bottleneck
Class selectors are faster than descendant selectors
questions.explanation: In modern browsers, selector matching is rarely a performance bottleneck — styles are evaluated right-to-left efficiently.

questions.question: What does `font-display: swap` do?
options: Shows a fallback font immediately, swaps when custom font loads
questions.explanation: font-display: swap shows text in a fallback font immediately and swaps to the custom font when loaded, preventing invisible text.

questions.question: What is CSS tree-shaking?
options: Removing unused CSS rules from the final bundle
questions.explanation: CSS tree-shaking (or purging) removes unused CSS rules, reducing file size.

questions.question: Which tool measures CSS coverage in Chrome DevTools?
questions.explanation: The Coverage tab in DevTools shows which CSS (and JS) is used vs unused.

questions.question: Why should you avoid @import in CSS?
options: It blocks parallel downloads and delays rendering
questions.explanation: @import blocks stylesheets from downloading in parallel, delaying render. Use <link> instead.

questions.question: What is CSS minification?
options: Removing whitespace, comments, and redundant code to reduce file size
questions.explanation: Minification removes unnecessary characters (whitespace, comments, semicolons) without changing functionality.

questions.question: Which CSS feature helps with efficient style recalculation?
questions.explanation: The contain property tells the browser which parts of an element are independent, limiting style recalc scope.

questions.question: What is the recommended CSS file size for good performance?
questions.explanation: CSS files under 100KB are generally well-optimized. Files over 200KB are considered large.

questions.question: How does `will-change` affect performance?
options: It hints to the browser to prepare for changes, improving animation performance
questions.explanation: will-change hints the browser to prepare for element changes, promoting to GPU layers, but should be used sparingly.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `contain-intrinsic-size`?
options: Provides a placeholder size for content-visibility elements
questions.explanation: contain-intrinsic-size provides a placeholder size for elements with content-visibility: auto to maintain scroll position.

questions.question: Which approach loads non-critical CSS without blocking?
questions.explanation: Using rel="preload" with onload to switch to stylesheet loads CSS asynchronously without blocking render.

questions.question: What is the impact of too many CSS rules on performance?
options: Increased style calculation time and memory usage
questions.explanation: Thousands of CSS rules increase style recalculation time and memory, especially on page load.

questions.question: Which animation property triggers layout recalculation?
questions.explanation: Animating height triggers layout recalculations. transform and opacity are GPU-composited.

questions.question: What is the purpose of CSS code-splitting?
options: To make code harder to read
To load only the CSS needed for the current route/view
To split CSS into smaller files for organization
questions.explanation: CSS code-splitting loads only the styles needed for the current page or route, reducing initial payload.

questions.question: How can you reduce CSS file size in a framework like Tailwind?
options: Purge unused classes with a build step
questions.explanation: Purging removes unused utility classes, dramatically reducing Tailwind's production CSS size.
